# Studio Brief

*Front-door session output, 2026-07-02 — the machine-facing Phase-0 input, distilled from the concept doc beside it (`concept.md`). This is the shape Maya consumes.*

## What is this story about?

On the night before his first day of high school, a kid named Aiden decides to walk in already somebody by faking his way through the AI-guru trend with Orby, a cute AI-assistant avatar that's secretly feeding him the pitch. Aiden burns through a manic sandbox of half-understood AI tools and filters chasing a video that will define him before anyone at his new school gets the chance to. He never gets it. Instead, a real, glitchy, humiliating meltdown he never meant to record is the thing that finally goes viral — and rather than learning anything from it, he immediately starts trying to fake THAT too. A story about the difference between wanting to help people and wanting to be seen, told through a kid who can't tell which one he's chasing anymore.

## Who is this character?

**Aiden** — an incoming freshman, about to start 9th grade, trying to define his whole personality before anyone at his new school gets a vote. Design-bearing: two faces, switched on a hard cut. On-camera, the instant he's recording: huge, unblinking, camera-ready eyes and a too-wide showman's grin. Off-camera, the instant the light goes off: droopy, tired, twitchy real eyes and a nervous stammer. The performed confidence is a mask; the tell is instant.

**Orby** — Aiden's AI-assistant avatar, the cute "helpful chatbot mascot" he props up as a cohost. Reads adorable and helpful whenever Aiden is watching or recording. The instant Aiden looks away, Orby gets one glimpse of something else — demonic, deranged — before snapping back the moment Aiden turns around (one ghost-beat maximum, never a recurring jump-scare). Pitches Aiden his next content idea in a ringmaster/carnival-barker cadence; genuinely useful ideas and unhinged ones land in the identical chipper register. Orby is the personified algorithm, and the algorithm never turns itself off.

## What is the tone?

Sincere mockery aimed entirely inward. 90s Nicktoon gross-out (Ren & Stimpy: wet linework, extreme close-ups, unhinged proportions) collides with internet-culture dread by way of Bo Burnham's "Welcome to the Internet" (a ringmaster's chipper sales pitch; mundane and horrifying content delivered in the identical register). Adult Swim-adjacent — made for the generation that grew up on this exact cartoon style and is now living the algorithmic burnout the show is about.

### What this is NOT

- Not a takedown of real creators, tools, or brands — every genre cliché is generic and invented; nothing on screen parodies a specific named person, channel, or product.
- Not a kids' show wearing a kid-protagonist costume — the humor, gross-out, and darkness play at full adult strength; a kid lead doesn't mean a kid audience.
- Not black-and-white about "the internet is bad" — the piece stays genuinely admiring of people who actually share knowledge; the mockery lands only on hollow, value-free content-for-content's-sake.
- Not a growth story — Aiden does not learn a lesson and change. The ending is the machine getting smarter about him, not him getting wiser about the machine.
- Not jump-scare horror — Orby's demonic glimpse is a single, disciplined beat, never a recurring gag or a genuine horror sequence.
- Not glossy or clean — no anime-smooth linework, no 3D-rendered polish, no motion-graphics slickness. The line has to be wet, wrong, and alive.

## What is the format?

A single pilot episode, 60–90 seconds, one continuous escalating sequence contained to Aiden's bedroom the night before/morning of his first day of high school — no act breaks. A full mini-series (one YouTube-genre cliché per episode, building across a school year to a season finale) is scoped as a later brainstorm, contingent on this pilot proving out.

## What is the target medium?

YouTube — a genuine, published series attempt, not only a portfolio piece (it doubles as anima's second art-style/story test, outside the Pencil Test lineage). Standard 16:9 master; the pitch sequence and the glitch payoff are natural vertical cut-down candidates for a Shorts release later.

## What is the deadline?

TBD — no hard deadline. This is exploratory pipeline validation; Sean sets the pace.

## What are the non-negotiables?

- The joke is always on Aiden's own attempts — no specific real creator, brand, or platform is ever named or parodied; genre clichés stay generic and invented.
- Real AI tools/brands are never shown as literal trademarked UI or logos — Orby and every in-app AI tool Aiden uses are original, generic-parody designs.
- Orby reads cute and helpful only while Aiden is watching or recording; the instant Aiden looks away, Orby gets exactly one beat to go demonic/deranged before snapping back — capped at one clean instance in the pilot, never a recurring jump-scare.
- Orby pitches content ideas in a ringmaster/carnival-barker cadence; genuinely useful ideas and unhinged/dangerous ones are delivered in the identical chipper register — the tell lives in content, never in performance.
- Aiden's on-camera face (huge unblinking eyes, too-wide grin) and off-camera face (droopy, tired, twitchy, stammering) switch on a hard cut, never an eased morph — the performed-vs-real contrast is the character's whole design.
- The clip that goes viral at the end is the one thing Aiden never engineered — an accidental, raw, humiliating moment caught mid-glitch. None of his manufactured "AI guru" content is allowed to be the thing that pops off.
- No moral, no growth: the pilot ends with Aiden immediately trying to re-engineer "being real" as the next format to game — the treadmill keeps running.
- Register plays at full Ren & Stimpy gross-out and Adult Swim-adjacent darkness — not softened for a younger audience.
- Runtime is 60–90 seconds, contained to Aiden's room; only Aiden and Orby are on-screen "real" characters — any additional personas or entities appear only inside a screen (phone/laptop), never as another real person in the room.
